title: Payroll Services

narrative.executive_view: The investable unit is a non-PEO independent payroll bureau serving small and midsize employers under recurring processing agreements. AI can compress its exception, inquiry, reconciliation, and document work, but the observable AI investment and distribution power sit chiefly with scaled platforms. A local bureau may retain some near-term process savings, yet there is no public independent-bureau evidence that quantifies that retention. The mature risk is not that payroll tax work vanishes; it is that the platform, embedded accounting system, bank, or POS owner controls the customer relationship and captures the economics [S1][S2][S3][S4][S5].
narrative.target_archetype: The named archetype is a US independent payroll bureau with recurring managed payroll, payroll-tax filing/remittance support, and service staff for SMB and lower-middle-market employers. It excludes PEO/co-employment businesses, staffing, employer-of-record operations, pure payroll software vendors, and national public HCM platforms. This is the relevant acquirable operator rather than the whole code, but the supplied 100–499-employee count cannot be allocated to independents versus platform, software, or other operations from published sources. Coverage is therefore deliberately MISSING rather than asserted as a fraction of the supplied band.
narrative.how_ai_changes_work: AI and workflow automation can reduce repetitive payroll checking, anomaly detection, data gathering, client inquiries, report creation, and implementation support. ADP describes agentic AI for payroll roles, payroll anomaly validation, missing-tax-registration resolution, service, implementation, and development work [S1]. Those are meaningful task analogs, but the supplied 4-digit occupational mix is CPA-firm-heavy and omits the payroll-clerk occupation; the role fractions are bounded assumptions, not a measurement of the target archetype.
narrative.value_capture: Payroll fees are commonly structured as recurring processing fees with a base component and a per-employee or per-transaction component; Paychex describes that structure and Gusto publicly posts per-person pricing [S2][S4]. Recurrence may let a bureau keep efficiency initially, but it does not establish a 24-month retained-savings share. Platforms can package payroll with HR, accounting integrations, financing, and partner-channel incentives, while ADP and Paychex also earn material client-fund interest that a typical bureau may not replicate [S1][S2][S3]. The packet therefore leaves retained savings MISSING rather than converting this adverse competitive evidence into unsupported capture precision.
narrative.adoption_timing: ADP's current product roadmap documents AI-enabled payroll workflows and internal service and implementation use, demonstrating that the relevant capabilities are already commercial at a scaled provider [S1]. It does not measure effective AI use among independent bureaus, nor the time at which half of them will materially change the tasks represented in the labor estimate. Tool availability is not effective adoption. Both present adoption and time to 50% are consequently MISSING pending a bureau-specific survey or operating dataset.
narrative.buyability: The dataset produces a deterministic NAICS band count, but neither the Census-derived band nor public platform filings identify how many are independent recurring payroll bureaus or available sellers. No current, credible owner-age or succession study for the named archetype was located. Seller supply is therefore recorded as UNKNOWN, which is neutral by the frozen method rather than negative. Likewise, public platform acquisitions and market presence do not count as a demonstrated number of buyers for small independent-bureau acquisitions, so active-consolidator count is MISSING.
narrative.valuation: No reopened public source provides a defensible EBITDA purchase and exit multiple range for this exact independent-bureau archetype. Public HCM-company market values, platform acquisitions, and broad software multiples would not be valid substitutes for local bureau transactions. The entry and exit multiple inputs are both MISSING. The frozen finalizer will use its preregistered neutral and sensitivity treatment; that is a missing-evidence convention, not a claim of stable multiples or an assumed spread.
narrative.operator_survival: At mature AI adoption the base survival class is DISINTERMEDIATED for the acquired independent bureau: payroll remains a paid and accountable function, but product, data, compliance automation, and distribution can migrate to platforms. ADP serves over 1.1 million clients, is building AI agents for payroll work, operates broad SMB platforms, and earns interest on held client funds [S1]. Gusto's accountant program combines a multi-client dashboard, migration support, revenue sharing, and a large partner directory [S3]. IRS reporting-agent rules preserve a legitimate intermediary function but do not reserve it for a local bureau [S5]. PRESSURED and MELTING remain plausible alternatives because local niche service may persist while direct/embedded platforms could also remove the bureau's role.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: The principal uncertainty is archetype scope: the supplied role mix and implied EBITDA band are not a bureau census. A second uncertainty is whether a rolled-up bureau can preserve local-accountant referrals, state-compliance expertise, and client trust despite platform migration. Public evidence is strongest for the platform side, not for target-bureau economics. The extreme supplied labor-share figure has vintage and revenue-netting limitations, and the role allocation has an unusually large residual. These limitations widen the usefulness of the result and require target-level diligence before any investment decision.

sources.title: ADP 2025 Form 10-K
sources.what_it_supports: Scaled payroll-platform AI features and internal workflow use, SMB platform reach, client-fund interest economics, recurring client relationships, and payroll-tax service scope.

sources.title: Paychex 2025 Form 10-K
sources.what_it_supports: Payroll processing fee structure, client retention, client-fund obligations, and the scale of a major HCM/payroll platform; it is a platform proxy rather than independent-bureau evidence.

sources.title: Gusto Partner Program for Accountants
sources.what_it_supports: Platform capture of accountant-channel distribution through migration support, a multi-client dashboard, partner incentives, and partner-directory visibility.

sources.title: Gusto Pricing, Plans & Fees
sources.what_it_supports: Public payroll pricing in recurring base-plus-per-person form and payroll-related platform add-ons; a pricing-model proxy, not local-bureau contracts.

sources.title: IRS Reporting Agents File
sources.what_it_supports: Reporting-agent authorization, electronic filing and tax-deposit functions, and the fact that reporting agents are companies performing payroll services for other businesses.

ai_replaceable_share.rationale: The supplied 4-digit 5412 mix is not payroll-bureau-specific and omits the signature payroll-clerk occupation. Fractions are task-level bounded judgments only, informed by the task descriptions in the prompt and not by adoption, pricing, deal, or survival evidence.
caveats: The supplied 4-digit occupation mix is CPA-firm-heavy rather than a measured 541214 payroll-bureau mix.
The 0.2982 residual wage share likely contains payroll-processing and customer-service work but cannot be allocated from the supplied data.
The high supplied labor share has a stated vintage mismatch and may overstate the revenue base exposed to labor savings.

role_judgments.rationale: Reconciliations, research, and drafting can be compressed; professional judgment and exception accountability remain.
Structured posting, reconciliation, and document handling are automatable, with review retained.
The supplied tax-preparer role is not a clean payroll role; research and preparation compress, while interpretation remains.
Management review, client accountability, and control design are only lightly compressible.
Scheduling, information collection, and routine correspondence can be automated; sensitive exceptions remain human.
Operating leadership and escalation ownership are not primarily removable tasks.
General clerical intake, lookup, and routine updates are suitable for workflow automation with review.
Implementation analysis and migration support can be assisted, while complex multi-state setup remains.
Billing, posting, and routine account updates are structured tasks but require exception handling.
Supervision can use AI summaries but retains staffing, control, and escalation accountability.
Residual likely includes the omitted payroll-processing and support population; the broad range reflects the absence of a payroll-specific occupational census.

target_archetype.name: Independent recurring SMB payroll bureau with managed tax filing
inclusion_criteria: US independent operator providing recurring payroll processing to SMB or lower-middle-market employers.
Managed payroll-tax filing, deposit/remittance support, or reporting-agent-style services.
A paid operator relationship rather than a pure software subscription sold directly by a national platform.
exclusion_criteria: PEO, co-employment, employer-of-record, staffing, or temporary-help businesses.
Pure HCM/payroll software vendors and public national HCM platforms.
Accounting firms for which payroll is an ancillary service rather than the operating core.
target_archetype.selection_basis: This is the distinct lower-middle-market operating model whose process savings and independent survival can be evaluated. The deterministic NAICS band is broader; no reopened source allocates it to this archetype.

coverage.rationale: No source located a count or bounded share of the supplied $1–10M EBITDA band that is composed of independent recurring payroll bureaus meeting the inclusion criteria.
caveats: The deterministic 297-firm band is not relabeled as independent bureaus.

retained_savings_share_24m.rationale: Public sources establish recurring pricing and platform/channel pressure but do not measure the share of AI-created operating savings retained by the named independent bureau after 24 months of repricing, vendor costs, renewal, and competitive response.
caveats: A recurring fee model is not evidence of a retained-savings percentage.
Platform competition is considered here only as a reason evidence is required; it is not converted into an invented low value.

t50_years.rationale: No reopened study measures when 50% of independent recurring payroll bureaus will materially deploy AI in the scored work; platform product availability is insufficient for that measure.
caveats: ADP's disclosed AI deployment is a capability proxy, not target-archetype adoption timing.

current_adoption_pct.rationale: No reopened independent-bureau survey measured effective current adoption in the tasks represented by the role estimates.
caveats: Public product launches and announced AI features do not establish materially used adoption among independent bureaus.

historical_analogs.value: Scaled payroll platforms have repeatedly moved payroll processing toward cloud, automation, self-service, and now AI-enabled workflows; that supports capability plausibility but does not quantify independent-bureau diffusion.
historical_analogs.rationale: The public filings document the scaled platform evolution most relevant to the workflow, with an explicit bridge limitation to the local-bureau archetype.
caveats: This is not a measured historical adoption curve for independent payroll bureaus.

seller_supply_signal.rationale: No credible, current owner-age, succession, or sell-side inventory evidence specific to the named independent-bureau archetype was located. UNKNOWN is the frozen neutral signal, not a scarcity judgment.
caveats: Observed seller supply could differ by local market, technology stack, and client concentration.

active_consolidators.rationale: No reopened source identified and counted buyers demonstrably active in acquisitions of the named independent recurring payroll-bureau archetype. Major platforms are competitive context, not automatically qualifying local-bureau consolidators.
caveats: A transaction database or broker dataset could materially change this input.

buy_mult.rationale: No reopened, defensible transaction-comparable range was found for acquisition of the named independent payroll-bureau archetype.
caveats: Public-platform valuation and generic software multiples are not substitutes for private bureau transactions.

exit_mult.rationale: No reopened, defensible exit multiple range was found for a scaled independent payroll-bureau platform after a hold period.
caveats: The exit buyer, technology ownership, client retention, and regulatory capacity would materially affect an observed exit multiple.

pricing_structure.value: Recurring base-plus-per-employee, per-transaction, or per-processing-period service fees, often with adjacent HR and compliance add-ons.
pricing_structure.rationale: Paychex reports fixed fees per processing period, sometimes plus an employee or transaction fee, and Gusto publicly displays monthly per-person payroll pricing. Both are scaled-platform proxies for the target bureau's common pricing architecture.
caveats: This is not a sample of target-bureau contracts, renewal clauses, or local price realization.

operator_survival.rationale: The payroll function remains accountable and technically mediated by reporting-agent processes, but the sources show AI capability, client-fund economics, direct pricing, and accountant-channel tooling concentrated in platforms. The evidence is a disclosed bridge from platform structure to the independent-bureau operator, so DISINTERMEDIATED is a base scenario rather than a direct observation.
caveats: IRS reporting-agent authorization does not confer exclusivity on local bureaus.
Niche, high-touch, or complex-state bureaus may remain PRESSURED rather than disintermediated.
Embedded payroll or direct self-service could produce a MELTING local-bureau outcome in some segments.

cross_checks.uplift_vs_timing_consistent: Task fractions state capability to remove or compress work, while adoption is MISSING because platform capability does not establish material independent-bureau use; no timing judgment was used to increase any role fraction.
cross_checks.capture_vs_survival_separated: Capture is strictly the unknown 24-month retained share of operating savings. Survival is the mature allocation of operator control and paid role; platform/channel evidence informs both only through these separately stated mechanisms.
cross_checks.no_deal_evidence_in_VA: No acquisition, valuation, buyer, channel, pricing, or survival evidence was used to set the role-level task fractions. Those fractions are flagged as occupational-mix assumptions.
cross_checks.multiple_neutrality_acknowledged: Missing buy and exit multiples receive the frozen neutral computational treatment only; this packet does not assert flat multiples, multiple expansion, or multiple compression.

dataset_mismatch.description: The deterministic labor share combines 2024 wages with 2022 receipts and is exceptionally high for the code; the supplied 4-digit 5412 occupational mix is CPA-firm-heavy and excludes the payroll-clerk signature occupation. The deterministic EBITDA band is an industry-level size-class estimate, not an independent-bureau count.
sensitivity: Role-replaceability bounds explicitly widen the large residual wage share.
Target-archetype coverage is MISSING rather than applying the full deterministic band to independent bureaus.
Target-level revenue, payroll, occupational mix, and EBITDA data are required before underwriting savings.

market_boundary.selection_basis: The selection is the independent recurring payroll-bureau operator because it is the acquirable service intermediary. PEO, staffing, EOR, and software-platform economics have different revenue, liability, and customer-control structures and are not treated as target equivalents.

top_uncertain_inputs.range: MISSING; no independent-bureau 24-month retention range located
top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: Determines whether process savings remain with the acquired operator after repricing, vendor cost, and competitive response.

top_uncertain_inputs.range: MISSING; no allocation of the 297-firm deterministic band to independent bureaus
top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: Determines the effective addressable target pool rather than assuming the full NAICS band is acquirable.

top_uncertain_inputs.range: MELTING, DISINTERMEDIATED, or PRESSURED are plausible
top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: Determines whether a paid payroll intermediary remains and whether the local operator or a platform controls its economics.

top_uncertain_inputs.range: MISSING; no target-archetype transaction ranges located
top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: Determines the entry-to-exit valuation effect without substituting public-platform values.

reviewer_note: This packet intentionally leaves target coverage, 24-month savings retention, adoption timing, active buyers, and both transaction-multiple inputs MISSING. The central research claim is structural: platform AI, funds economics, direct pricing, and accountant channels can disintermediate a local payroll bureau even where payroll compliance remains necessary.
